figures.caption: Fig. 6: Pairwise scatterplots to compare the solve times of two tools on a benchmark level. The axes are in log-log format, meaning that only slight deviations from the main diagonal already constitute a large difference. In particular, the dashed lines already mark a difference by a factor of 2. Note, that for the sake of readability, these plots start their axes at 1, cutting of some samples. The only notable samples that are missing are the “direct strategy” samples that LtlSynt solves in under a second whereas SemML, (as well as Strix) timeouts.
Fig. 5: Confusion matrices to highlight unique and overlapping solves for every pair of tools

Fig. 1: Structural overview of SemML’s high-level architecture. All items marked by + are novel or significant improvements over our prior version. Additionally, several minor engineering improvements have been added to all stages.

Fig. 3b. Observe, that without the semantic analysis, our Zielonka automaton has an extra state, and thus the resulting product DPA would be respectively larger. Finally, we depict the resulting DPA in Fig. 4. The labelling of this DPA is given by the labelling of the DELA (a boolean combination of tuples of LTL formulae), and the Zielonka leaf.

Fig. 8: Pairwise scatterplots to compare the solution sizes of two tools on a benchmark level. The axes are in log-log format, meaning that only slight deviations from the main diagonal already constitute a large difference. In particular, the dashed lines already mark a difference by a factor of 10.

Fig. 7: Speedup factors for all tracks when taking different lower cutoffs. The number in a cell is calculated by taking the geometric mean of runtime ratios (SemML over row-tool) over all benchmarks where at least one tool required at least column many seconds to solve. In particular, we cut out benchmarks where both tools required less than column many seconds to focus on complex samples. The number in brackets denotes the number of remaining benchmarks used to calculate this geometric mean over, i.e. the number of benchmarks, (i) solved by at least one tool and (ii) at least one tool ran longer than the column’s value in seconds. When a tool ran into the 30 minute timeout, we use that timeout value for the runtime ratio, which is an approximation that benefits tools with more timeouts
